title: Input and output
steps.0.text: Make a function called `withVat`. It takes a price and returns the price with tax, rounded. Exactly that name — the checks look for it.
steps.1.text: Call it three times, with the numbers 12, 30 and 7. Print the result of each.
steps.2.text: The two lines with the sum should exist in only one place in the whole file.
checkLabels.t13: The function is called three times
why.untouched: script.js is exactly as it was.
hint2.untouched: The same two lines, written three times with a different number.
why.not-linked: The script never reaches the page.
why.crashed: Your code stopped with an error.
hint2.crashed: Look at the Console panel below.
hint3.crashed: ReferenceError: withVat is not defined means the function either does not exist or is called something else. The name has to be exactly withVat.
hint2.no-function: The sum is written once and given a name. Then it is called with different numbers.
hint2.still-repeating: Both lines go inside the function, and only once.
why.wrong-function: The function does not give the right answer.
hint2.wrong-function: Twelve with twenty percent tax is 14.4, rounded 14.
hint3.wrong-function: If you get undefined, the function calculates but does not return. The result has to come back out.
hint4.wrong-function: return sends the value back to whoever called the function.
why.wrong-general: The function does not work with an arbitrary number.
hint2.wrong-general: The number should not be written inside. It comes from outside, through the brackets.
hint3.wrong-general: The name in the function's brackets is a blank space. It is filled with whatever you pass when calling it.
why.not-called: The function exists, but it is never called.
hint2.not-called: The old sum is still there. The three lines have to go through withVat.
hint3.not-called: console.log(withVat(12)); — the number goes into the function's brackets, the result comes back out.
why.wrong-count: There are not three lines in the console.
why.lost-p: A paragraph has disappeared from the page.
blocks.1.text: Three totals with tax. The same two lines, written three times with a different number.
blocks.2.text: Now imagine the tax becomes nineteen percent. Or that there are a hundred totals. Or that somebody asks for rounding down instead. Three places to fix, then a hundred, then a hundred and one — because you missed one.
blocks.4.text: You write the sum once, give it a name, and then call it as many times as you like with different numbers.
blocks.5.marks.0.label: KEYWORD
blocks.5.marks.1.label: NAME
blocks.5.marks.2.label: PARAMETER
blocks.5.legend.0: The highlighted part is the body. It runs only when the function is called.
blocks.6.items.0: `function` — a word of the language. It says a function is being declared here.
blocks.6.items.1: `withVat` — the name. This is what you call it by later. You choose it.
blocks.6.items.2: `price` in the brackets — the *parameter*. A blank space that gets a value only at the call.
blocks.6.items.3: Between the curly brackets — the body. The lines that run.
blocks.7.text: Which of these words belong to the language and which did you choose? Only `function`, `const` and `return` are the language. Everything else is yours — the same function works like this too:
blocks.9.text: It works exactly the same. Names are for the person reading the code, not for the browser. That is why they are chosen to mean something.
blocks.10.text: Writing it does not run it
blocks.11.text: Declare a function and nothing happens. It sits and waits. The console stays silent.
blocks.13.text: It runs only when you call it — with its name and brackets, and inside the brackets the number you are passing.
blocks.17.items.0: You call `withVat(12)`. The number 12 goes into the brackets.
blocks.17.items.4: `return` sends 14 back out. Where you called it, `withVat(12)` is now 14.
blocks.18.text: Pass a different number and the same five steps run with it:
blocks.20.text: One body, two different answers. That is exactly why the parameter exists.
blocks.21.text: The brackets mean do it now
blocks.22.text: The name without brackets and the name with brackets are two different things:
blocks.24.text: The first gives you the function itself, unperformed. The second runs it and gives the result. The same mistake as with string methods in lesson thirteen.
blocks.26.text: You will meet the explanation that a function is a piece of code you saved for later. That is true, and useless.
blocks.27.text: A function is something that takes an input and returns an output. Give it 12, get 14. Give it 30, get 36. What it does inside is its own business.
blocks.28.quote: Think of it as a machine with a funnel on top and a spout underneath.
blocks.30.items.0: A function is declared once and called as many times as needed.
blocks.30.items.1: The parameter in the brackets is a blank space filled in at the call.
blocks.30.items.2: Declaring runs nothing. The brackets do.
blocks.30.items.4: You choose the name. The language gives only `function`, `const` and `return`.
